data.title: Meridian Holdings Inc reports results for the quarter ended March 31 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Meridian Holdings Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 51 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended March 31. The mean expectation of three analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 2 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from -4 cents to 7 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 16% to $49.58 million from a year ago; analysts expected $52.84 million.

  • Meridian Holdings Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was 2 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $2.26 million.

  • Meridian Holdings Inc shares had held steady so far this quarter and lost 24.9% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had fallen by about 62.5% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no negative revisions of earnings estimates

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 2 "strong buy" or "buy," no "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the online services peer group is "hold."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Meridian Holdings Inc is $17.00, about 57.5% above its last closing price of $7.22

data.title: EssilorLuxottica mulled Amplifon investment, sees audio as key growth area
data.description:
Franco-Italian eyewear giant EssilorLuxottica has in the past looked at investing in Italian hearing aid maker Amplifon , CEO Francesco Milleri said on Tuesday, adding that audio will be among the group's biggest growth areas.

"We monitor almost everything that happens within our world, so we also looked at Amplifon," Milleri said when asked if EssilorLuxottica had considered taking part in a past capital hike by the hearing aid maker.

He did not specify which capital increase he was referring to or the size of the stake that was considered.

"Then they (Amplifon) made different choices", the CEO said on the sidelines of EssilorLuxottica's annual shareholders meeting. The group continues to monitor potential M&A transactions around the world, he said.

Earlier this year Amplifon agreed to buy the hearing business of Denmark's GN Store Nord for 2.3 billion euros ($2.69 billion), in a deal that created a group spanning both manufacturing and retail distribution.

EssilorLuxottica is already present in the audio sector with its Nuance Audio glasses, a type of eyewear incorporating hearing assistance for people with mild to moderate hearing loss.

Growth in the audio segment will come "through new products that will enhance Nuance’s performance, while also expanding our portfolio with innovative products offering different characteristics," Milleri said.

($1 = 0.8555 euros)

data.title: UAE quits OPEC. Here's what it means for the oil prices and the economy.
data.description:
By Jules Rimmer

The United Arab Emirates will withdraw from the OPEC and OPEC+ oil cartels on May 1, 2026 state media said on April 28, 2026, calling it a strategic decision by the major producer.

The decision by the United Arab Emirates on Tuesday to quit the Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries is a sign the cartel is losing its grip on the ability to control the worldwide supply and price of oil.

The UAE move, effective May 1, was explained by its energy ministry as an "evolution of sector policies to enhance flexibility in responding to market dynamics, while continuing to contribute to market stability in a thoughtful and responsible manner."

The UAE isn't the first to leave OPEC - Qatar did in 2019 - and some, like Ecuador, returned after leaving. But the decision to exit at this crucial juncture in the oil market will have major repercussions.

Increasing production on their own terms, rather than strictly limited by membership of a body with many competing interests, will give the Emiratis much greater flexibility.

As and when the Iran war ends, the seven emirates comprising the federation will have significant refunding commitments to rebuild the damage on their economies caused by the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. The more output there is, the greater the pressure there will be on prices to fall.

"I think it shows the cartel doesn't have same influence with Venezuela now a de facto U.S. puppet and that the U.S. is now the global swing producer. UAE will benefit from being able to align itself closer with U.S. now," said Ashley Kelty, an energy analyst at Panmure Liberum.

It also comes after the U.S. and the UAE discussed the possibility of a swap line being extended to the Gulf state.

Oil futures (BRN00) (CL00) briefly dipped, but recovered, after the news came out.

"Though the UAE have pledged to 'gradually' increase production after their departure, it goes without saying that actually doing so at present is somewhere between difficult, and impossible," said Michael Brown, senior research strategist at Pepperstone.

-Jules Rimmer

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: GM to Get $500 Million Tariff Refund, Beats Wall Street Expectations — WSJ
data.description:
By Christopher Otts and Nicholas G. Miller

General Motors easily topped Wall Street estimates for profitability in the first quarter thanks to lower costs from its electric-vehicle pullback and an expected tariff refund following the Supreme Court's rejection of President Trump's emergency tariffs.

Revenue for the U.S.'s largest automaker was down less than 1% from the prior year, reflecting minimal impact so far from the Iran conflict and elevated U.S. gasoline prices on vehicle sales. GM sold fewer electric vehicles and about the same number of gas-powered vehicles as a year ago.

GM's Chevrolet Silverado full-size pickup saw an 8% sales bump, while the company recorded its highest sales to fleet customers like businesses and government units since 2020.

"We have solid momentum in our core operations," Chief Executive Mary Barra said in her quarterly letter to shareholders.

GM reported $4.2 billion in adjusted earnings in the quarter, well ahead of the expected $3 billion, according to FactSet. The result was helped by an expected tariff refund of $500 million and another $1 billion of costs related to its EV reset that the automaker excluded as a special item.

GM now expects gross tariff costs of $2.5 billion to $3.5 billion in 2026, down from its original estimate of $3 billion to $4 billion.

The company boosted its full-year adjusted earnings guidance to $11.50 to $13.50 a share, up from its previous forecast of $11 to $13 a share.

Analysts see full-year adjusted earnings of $12.20 a share.

Shares rose 4.7% to $81.60 in premarket trading.

For the first quarter, the company posted net income of $2.71 billion, down from $2.85 billion the year prior. On a per-share basis, earnings fell to $2.82 from last year's $3.35.

Adjusted earnings were $3.70 a share. Analysts polled by FactSet had expected $2.60 a share.

Revenue ticked down 0.9% to $43.62 billion. Wall Street had expected $43.51 billion.

Write to Christopher Otts at christopher.otts@wsj.com and Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com
